draft.introduction: Matthew 12:1–14 presents the Sabbath controversies, where Jesus declares himself “lord of the Sabbath.” The passage raises questions about the relationship between divine law and divine presence. From Codex Sinaiticus.

translation.text: At that time Jesus went through the grainfields on the Sabbath. His disciples were hungry and began to pluck heads of grain and eat. But when the Pharisees saw it, they said to him, “Look, your disciples are doing what is not lawful to do on the Sabbath.”
translation.original_text: εν εκεινω τω καιρω επορευθη ο ιησους τοις σαββασιν δια των σποριμων

translation.text: I tell you, something greater than the temple is here. But if you had known what this means, “I desire mercy and not sacrifice,” you would not have condemned the guiltless. For the Son of Man is lord of the Sabbath.
translation.original_text: λεγω δε υμιν οτι του ιερου μειζον εστιν ωδε

translator_notes.note: The claim that “something greater than the temple is here” is among the most audacious in the Synoptic Gospels. The temple was the locus of God’s presence on earth; to claim superiority over it implies a new and greater mode of divine indwelling.
